diagrams.title: CAP Theorem Triangle
diagrams.description: Visual representation showing the three properties of CAP theorem with regions showing which combinations are achievable
elements: Consistency vertex at top
Availability vertex on left
Partition Tolerance vertex on right
Central region showing CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance)
Left region showing AP (Availability + Partition Tolerance)
Right region showing CA (Consistency + Availability) - not achievable in practice
Note: Partition tolerance is mandatory in wide-area systems
diagrams.purpose: Help students visualize that during partition, must choose between C and A, and that partitions are inevitable making CA non-viable

diagrams.title: Two-Phase Commit State Diagram
diagrams.description: State machine showing 2PC protocol flow with coordinator and participant states, highlighting blocking scenario
elements: Coordinator states: Init, Prepare, Commit, Abort, Recovery
Participant states: Init, Prepared, Committed, Aborted
Prepare message arrow from coordinator to participants
Yes/No vote arrows from participants to coordinator
Commit/Abort arrows from coordinator to participants
Dotted box highlighting 'Blocking Region' after Prepare phase
X mark showing where coordinator failure causes blocking
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate the protocol flow and identify exactly where blocking occurs, helping students understand why coordinator failure during 'Prepared' state causes indefinite wait

diagrams.title: Lamport Timestamp Ordering
diagrams.description: Three processes exchanging messages showing how Lamport logical clocks capture happened-before relationship
elements: Process P1 timeline with events e1, e2, e4
Process P2 timeline with events e3, e5
Process P3 timeline with events e6
Message arrows showing causal dependencies
Timestamp labels at each event (e.g., L=0, L=1, etc.)
Dotted arrows showing message carries timestamp
Legend explaining 'happens-before' → relationship
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrate how logical clocks provide partial ordering of events and how messages help propagate time across processes
